work at a convenience store and you're driving to work this morning, your car's making a funny noise. You may just, when you leave work, stop at whatever place is close. If you've got your mobile phone with you while you're supposed to be working, you might Google auto repair, you might look through the, oh here's a coupon, there's all sorts of ways you might kind of almost indiscriminately pick to get your problem with the car fixed, right? You're now driving to your office in your Bentley. Right. And it's making a funny noise. If you don't already have somebody to take care of it, there's somebody in your Rolodex you call and you say, who takes care of your car? So the first impulse of an affluent person is friend, relative, neighbor, peer who would know, right? So, you know, Donald Trump's hairstylist drops dead and he needs a hairstylist. He's not using a coupon out of Valpac. Right. It's not happening. He's calling somebody and saying, who do you go to? All of a sudden my person ran off to Bermuda with her boyfriend. Where do you go? So this makes it more necessary to do this well. But it also makes it infinitely more productive to do it well. And furthermore, toward the top end, now not massive fluid, not the Neiman Marcus shopper versus the Kohl shopper, but levels above that. They form real clans, kind of gated communities, not necessarily physically, but and what happens is if you penetrate it with one, they have this huge formidable defense, but once you're inside it, they have no defenses at all. They are 100% susceptible to referral. That's why a thing like Bernie Madoff happens again and again, although Bernie did it more magnificently at the high end than just about anybody else. But it's why it happens and why it's basically impossible to stop because once he penetrates the defenses of the small clan of let's say super affluent CEOs of university endowment funds, of which there's maybe, I don't know, 500, 1000, if that many. Once he's in with one, he is accepted, embraced and unquestioned by all the rest of them because they know they have this formidable defense they've built. And so he now runs amok, all right. And he doesn't have to get him directly necessarily. All he's got to do is be seed with the one he out. Right. Right. Wow. Powerful. All right. Dikini says we saw that. Not suggesting that anybody should perpetrate investment fraud. That's not what I was suggesting. Well, I want to make the lawyers happy. Thank you for the recommendation. No, it was an example. There was an example. All right. We've got time for one more quick question here. We sell furniture in Cabo San Lucas, Mexico. My office is in the U.S. How would I go about firing up a referral program primarily online? Well, again, A, I'm not sure that that's the right question. Yeah, I'm not sure it's the right question. Okay. And I would have questions. Are they in Cabo? Are they coming into a physical location? Is there a staff there? Are there sales clerks there? Are people buying online and having it shipped? So there's questions. What I would say that might be instructive is to understand that you don't fire this up just technically. Right. This is not an answer of just mechanics. Right. This is an answer of relationship. And so that the question has to really be about how do I create relationship with these customers that allows me to then fire up?
